content.problemStatement:
Suppose that we have a rooted tree TTT with vertices 1,…,n1, \ldots, n1,…,n. We construct an undirected ancestor graph GGG of TTT with the same set of vertices. A pair of distinct vertices v,uv, uv,u is adjacent in GGG if and only if vvv is an ancestor of uuu in TTT, or vice versa.

You are given a graph GGG with nnn vertices. Construct any rooted tree TTT such that GGG is the ancestor graph of TTT, or determine that no such TTT exists.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer QQQ, the number of test cases. QQQ test case descriptions follow.
Each test case description starts with a line containing two integers, nnn and mmm, the number of vertices and edges of the graph GGG respectively.
The iii-th of the following mmm lines describes the iii-th edge of GGG. It contains two integers, ui,viu_i, v_iui​,vi​ (1≤ui,vi≤n1 \leq u_i, v_i \leq n1≤ui​,vi​≤n), the endpoints of the iii-th edge.


Output Format
For each test case:



If there is no suitable rooted tree TTT, print a single line containing the word `NO`.
Otherwise, on the first line print `YES`, and in the next line, print nnn integers p1,…,pnp_1, \ldots, p_np1​,…,pn​ describing the tree TTT. pip_ipi​ should be equal to the index of the parent vertex of iii, or 000 if vertex iii is the root. The numbers should describe a valid rooted tree, that is, there should be exactly one root vertex which should be an ancestor of any other vertex. If there are multiple suitable trees, output any of them.
constraints: The graphs do not contain self-loops or multiple edges.
content.inputFormat: First line contains an integer Q, the number of test cases. Each test case contains two integers, n and m, the number of vertices and edges of the graph respectively. The next m lines describe the edges of the graph, each containing two integers, u and v, the endpoints of the edge.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print 'YES' if a suitable rooted tree exists, otherwise print 'NO'. If a tree exists, print the indices of the parent vertices of each vertex in the tree, separated by spaces.

solution.approach: We use a depth-first search (DFS) to construct the ancestor graph. We start from an arbitrary vertex and explore all its neighbors. We then recursively explore all the neighbors of the current vertex. We use a stack to keep track of the vertices to visit. We also use a map to keep track of the parent of each vertex. If we encounter a cycle, we print 'NO'. Otherwise, we print 'YES' and the indices of the parent vertices.
